specification.topic-statement: The keyboard input parser translates a stream of raw TTY bytes into semantic input actions one byte at a time, handling UTF-8 multibyte characters and ANSI escape sequences through two independent stateful sub-parsers.

scope.in-scope: All 28 semantic action types, the byte routing decision tree, UTF-8 decoding with overlong and surrogate rejection, legacy control character mapping, printable ASCII fast path, escape sequence buffering and dispatch, legacy CSI arrow and tilde sequences, SGR mouse scroll sequences, CSI u protocol, shifted character translation via keyboard layout, and error recovery.
scope.boundary: What happens after an action is produced — cursor movement, history navigation, text insertion, etc. — is handled by the REPL action dispatcher and is out of scope.

Bytes injected programmatically by the key injection subsystem are fed to this same parser. Whether a byte originates from the TTY or from injection is indistinguishable at the parser level and is out of scope here.

field.name: type
field.note: one of 28 semantic action types; see behavior section for the complete list

field.name: codepoint
field.note: the Unicode codepoint for the character action type only; zero for all other action types

field.name: escape buffer
field.note: accumulates bytes of an in-progress escape sequence; indexed by escape length

field.name: escape length
field.note: number of bytes currently in the escape buffer

field.name: in escape
field.note: true while collecting an escape sequence

field.name: UTF-8 buffer
field.note: accumulates bytes of an in-progress multibyte UTF-8 sequence

field.name: UTF-8 length
field.note: bytes accumulated so far for the current UTF-8 sequence

field.name: UTF-8 expected
field.note: total bytes required to complete the current UTF-8 sequence (2, 3, or 4)

field.name: in UTF-8
field.note: true while collecting a multibyte UTF-8 sequence

behavior.semantic-actions:
The parser produces exactly one of 28 action types per byte. Most actions carry no payload. The character action carries a Unicode codepoint.
Actions with no payload: - Newline (Enter key — submit the current line) - Insert newline (Shift/Ctrl/Alt+Enter — insert a newline without submitting) - Backspace - Delete (forward delete) - Arrow left, arrow right, arrow up, arrow down - Page up, page down - Scroll up, scroll down (mouse wheel) - Ctrl+A (move to line start) - Ctrl+C (interrupt) - Ctrl+E (move to line end) - Ctrl+K (kill to end of line) - Ctrl+N (next history entry) - Ctrl+P (previous history entry) - Ctrl+U (kill entire line) - Ctrl+W (delete word backward) - Tab (completion trigger) - Escape - Navigate to previous sibling agent (Ctrl+Left) - Navigate to next sibling agent (Ctrl+Right) - Navigate to parent agent (Ctrl+Up) - Navigate to child agent (Ctrl+Down) - Unknown (byte was valid but produced no semantic result, or sequence is incomplete)
The character action carries a codepoint and represents a typeable Unicode character — printable ASCII or any valid non-control Unicode codepoint.
behavior.byte-routing:
Each byte received is routed through the following decision tree in strict order. Only one path executes per byte.
1. If currently collecting a UTF-8 continuation sequence, attempt to append the byte as a continuation byte. See UTF-8 decoding.
2. If currently collecting an escape sequence, dispatch the byte to the escape sequence parser. See escape sequence parsing.
3. If the byte is `0x1B` (ESC), enter escape collection mode (escape length reset to zero, in-escape set to true) and return Unknown.
4. If the byte matches a control character listed in the legacy control character table below, return the corresponding action immediately.
5. If the byte is in the range `0x20`–`0x7E` inclusive, return a character action with the byte value as the codepoint (printable ASCII fast path). Exception: byte `0x7F` (DEL) is treated as Backspace, not a printable character.
6. If the byte is a UTF-8 lead byte (`0xC0`–`0xF7`), enter UTF-8 collection mode. See UTF-8 decoding.
7. All other bytes produce Unknown with no state change.
behavior.legacy-control-characters:
The following byte values map directly to actions without entering any state machine:
- `0x01` → Ctrl+A - `0x03` → Ctrl+C - `0x05` → Ctrl+E - `0x09` → Tab - `0x0A` → Insert newline - `0x0B` → Ctrl+K - `0x0D` → Newline - `0x0E` → Ctrl+N - `0x10` → Ctrl+P - `0x15` → Ctrl+U - `0x17` → Ctrl+W - `0x7F` → Backspace
All other byte values in the ranges `0x00`–`0x08`, `0x0C`, `0x0F`–`0x12`, `0x13`–`0x14`, `0x16`, `0x18`–`0x1A` produce Unknown.
behavior.utf8-decoding:
UTF-8 multibyte sequences are decoded incrementally. Lead bytes are recognized by bit pattern:
- `110xxxxx` (range `0xC0`–`0xDF`): 2-byte sequence - `1110xxxx` (range `0xE0`–`0xEF`): 3-byte sequence - `11110xxx` (range `0xF0`–`0xF7`): 4-byte sequence
When a lead byte is detected, UTF-8 collection mode is entered: the byte is stored, the expected total is set (2, 3, or 4), and Unknown is returned.
Each subsequent byte is tested as a continuation byte: it must match the pattern `10xxxxxx` (value in `0x80`–`0xBF`). If the pattern does not match, UTF-8 mode is exited immediately and Unknown is returned; the offending byte is discarded without being re-processed.
When the collected byte count reaches the expected total, the codepoint is assembled by extracting the data bits from each byte. The assembled codepoint is then validated:
- Overlong encodings (a 2-byte encoding of a codepoint below U+0080, a 3-byte encoding below U+0800, or a 4-byte encoding below U+10000) produce U+FFFD (replacement character) instead of the encoded value. - UTF-16 surrogate codepoints (U+D800 through U+DFFF) produce U+FFFD. - Codepoints above U+10FFFF produce U+FFFD.
All valid or replaced codepoints are returned as a character action. UTF-8 collection mode is then exited.
When a non-continuation byte is received while in UTF-8 mode, only the mode is reset — the bad byte is not reprocessed. It is silently discarded, and the next byte is treated as the start of a new sequence.
behavior.escape-sequence-parsing:
When the ESC byte (`0x1B`) is received outside of escape mode, the parser enters escape collection mode and returns Unknown. Subsequent bytes are appended to the escape buffer (up to 15 bytes). On each byte, the buffer is tested against the patterns below in order. When a pattern matches completely, the corresponding action is returned and escape mode is exited.
The first byte after ESC determines the sequence class:
- `[` (0x5B): begins a CSI sequence; continue collecting. - ESC (`0x1B`): a second consecutive ESC produces the Escape action and exits escape mode. - Any other byte: exit escape mode and return Unknown.
A bare ESC byte followed by nothing (or by a non-recognized byte) does not produce the Escape action. The Escape action is produced only by a double ESC or by the CSI u encoding `ESC [ 27 ; 1 u`. Terminals that send a bare ESC for the Escape key are not supported without using CSI u mode.
Four 3-byte sequences `ESC [ A`, `ESC [ B`, `ESC [ C`, `ESC [ D` (final bytes 0x41–0x44) map to arrow up, down, right, and left respectively.
Sequences of the form `ESC [ 1 ; N A/B/C/D` carry a modifier number N. The NumLock bit (bit 7, value 128) is masked off before interpreting N. Only modifier 5 (Ctrl) is handled; it maps the four arrow directions to the four agent-navigation actions (Ctrl+Up/Down/Left/Right). All other modifier values produce Unknown.
Sequences beginning with `ESC [ <` and ending with `M` (press) are parsed as SGR mouse sequences. The button number is extracted from the parameter before the first semicolon. Button 64 produces scroll up; button 65 produces scroll down. All other SGR mouse sequences produce Unknown.
Selected sequences ending with `~` or matching specific Home/End forms map to actions:
- `ESC [ H` and `ESC [ 1 ~` and `ESC [ 1 ; N H` → Ctrl+A (line start) - `ESC [ F` and `ESC [ 4 ~` and `ESC [ 1 ; N F` → Ctrl+E (line end) - `ESC [ 3 ~` → Delete - `ESC [ 5 ~` → Page up - `ESC [ 6 ~` → Page down
Modifier values in Home/End sequences are accepted but not further distinguished; they all map to the same line-boundary action.
Sequences of the form `ESC [ keycode ; modifiers u` implement the CSI u keyboard protocol. The keycode and modifiers are parsed as decimal integers. The NumLock bit (bit 7) is masked off the modifier before use.
Modifier values: - 1: no modifier - 2: Shift - 5: Ctrl
Keycode/modifier combinations and their actions:
- 13 / 1 → Newline - 13 / 2, 3, 5, 6 → Insert newline - 9 / 1 → Tab - 27 / 1 → Escape - 127 / 1 → Backspace - 97 / 5 → Ctrl+A - 99 / 5 → Ctrl+C - 101 / 5 → Ctrl+E - 107 / 5 → Ctrl+K - 110 / 5 → Ctrl+N - 112 / 5 → Ctrl+P - 117 / 5 → Ctrl+U - 119 / 5 → Ctrl+W - 32–126 / 1 → Character action with the keycode as codepoint - 32–126 / 2 → Character action with the shifted translation of the keycode - Any keycode above 50000 → Unknown - All other combinations → Unknown
For shifted characters (modifier 2, keycode 32–126), a keyboard layout lookup is performed to determine what character would be produced by pressing that key with Shift held. If the lookup fails for any reason (layout not available, keycode not found, result codepoint is zero), the original keycode is returned as the codepoint unchanged.
Sequences that end with `m` (SGR color and style codes) are silently discarded; they produce Unknown. Certain short tilde sequences not listed in the tilde table above are also silently discarded. These are recognized so that terminal color output interleaved with key sequences does not leave the parser in a permanently stuck escape state.
If 15 bytes have been collected in the escape buffer and no recognized sequence has terminated, the next byte triggers overflow handling: the escape buffer is reset, escape mode is exited, and Unknown is returned. No bytes are processed after the overflow; the overflowing byte is discarded.
behavior.state-independence: The escape sub-parser and the UTF-8 sub-parser are mutually exclusive at runtime: the byte routing decision tree checks in-UTF-8 mode first. In practice, a valid terminal will never interleave UTF-8 continuation bytes with escape sequences, so this ordering produces correct results without any explicit conflict detection.
